---
title: 'The Freedom Center --> This is just a church... isn''t it? Sigh....'
date: '2025-01-30T04:00:00.0000000+00:00'
draft: false
categories:
  - 'psychology'
  - 'counseling'
  - 'Biblical Studies'
  - 'Freedom'
  - 'Armchair Theology'
---

<div>I'm the guy who keeps desperately trying to NOT be a "pastor" has this idea. While specifics have changes, this core idea has been growing since at least 2003.... "It's not a church, it's...."</div><div><br /></div><div>I looked at this summary below of my ideas (but better organized by AI) and thought to myself,</div><div><br /></div><div><i><blockquote>"This is a church, isn't it?&nbsp;</blockquote><blockquote>There is no concert followed by a public speaker (although I could see how that could happen too)...&nbsp;</blockquote><blockquote>This is just a different expression of, and reimagining of, a church in a new format. A "<a href="https://freshexpressions.com/" target="_blank">Fresh Expression</a>". I would want to make sure the format and resources were always free, like the <a href="https://bibleproject.com/" target="_blank">BibleProject </a>has modeled.</blockquote><blockquote>But at the heart, this is just a church isn't it?&nbsp;</blockquote><blockquote>After all that running... Am I just "pastor" hiding behind other titles? Ha ha... Sigh...."</blockquote></i></div><div><br /></div><div>Well... I'll just sit with that feeling for now. I'm not sure what to do with it. I don't have to "make" anything happen. The idea has been percolating since at least 2003, it can keep percolating until the path becomes clear. But... I'm really afraid this is a church. Ha ha ha...&nbsp;</div><div><br /></div><div><br /></div><div><h4>AI Response (DeepSeek) - 2:</h4></div><div><p><strong>The Freedom Center</strong>&nbsp;is a fantastic name—it evokes liberation, growth, and exploration, which aligns perfectly with your vision. Let’s refine and expand the concept with this name in mind, ensuring it captures the essence of what you’re trying to build.</p>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Mission Statement for The Freedom Center</strong></h3>
<p><em>"The Freedom Center is a community dedicated to fostering spiritual, intellectual, and emotional growth. We provide a safe space for individuals to explore ancient wisdom, develop critical thinking skills, and engage in meaningful dialogue—free from dogma, judgment, or pressure. Through interdisciplinary learning, therapeutic support, and communal reflection, we empower people to wrestle with big questions, grow in self-awareness, and apply timeless insights to modern life."</em></p>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Core Values of The Freedom Center</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Freedom</strong>: Liberation from rigid doctrines, creeds, and traditions; freedom to question, explore, and grow.</li>
<li><strong>Curiosity</strong>: A commitment to asking questions, seeking understanding, and embracing uncertainty.</li>
<li><strong>Community</strong>: A safe, inclusive space where people can share, learn, and grow together.</li>
<li><strong>Wisdom</strong>: Engaging with ancient texts and ideas as sources of meditative wisdom, not prescriptive rules.</li>
<li><strong>Integration</strong>: Blending spiritual, intellectual, and emotional growth into a holistic approach to life.</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Programs and Offerings</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Freedom Circles</strong>&nbsp;(Small Groups):
<ul>
<li><strong>Bible Exploration</strong>: Contextual, ANE/2nd Temple-focused readings of Scripture as wisdom literature.</li>
<li><strong>Wisdom Dialogues</strong>: Discussions on philosophical, theological, and psychological topics.</li>
<li><strong>Therapeutic Groups</strong>: Facilitated sessions focused on emotional and spiritual growth.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Freedom Labs</strong>&nbsp;(Workshops and Courses):
<ul>
<li><strong>Critical Thinking 101</strong>: Teaching skills for analyzing ideas, avoiding fallacies, and engaging in constructive dialogue.</li>
<li><strong>Ancient Wisdom for Modern Life</strong>: Applying biblical and philosophical insights to contemporary challenges.</li>
<li><strong>Emotional Intelligence</strong>: Workshops on self-awareness, empathy, and healthy communication.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Freedom Forums</strong>&nbsp;(Events and Guest Speakers):
<ul>
<li><strong>Scholar Series</strong>: Inviting biblical scholars, theologians, and philosophers to share their insights.</li>
<li><strong>Community Conversations</strong>: Open forums on topics like justice, forgiveness, love, and meaning.</li>
<li><strong>Creative Expression</strong>: Art, music, and writing workshops to explore spirituality and self-expression.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Freedom Therapy</strong>&nbsp;(Therapeutic Support):
<ul>
<li><strong>Lay Counseling</strong>: Trained facilitators offering non-clinical support for emotional and spiritual growth.</li>
<li><strong>Therapist Partnerships</strong>: Collaborating with licensed professionals for clinical needs.</li>
<li><strong>Support Groups</strong>: Focused on specific issues like grief, addiction, or relationships.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Freedom Online</strong>&nbsp;(Digital Resources):
<ul>
<li><strong>Resource Library</strong>: Articles, videos, and courses on biblical studies, critical thinking, and emotional growth.</li>
<li><strong>Virtual Freedom Circles</strong>: Online small groups for remote participants.</li>
<li><strong>Podcast/YouTube Channel</strong>: Sharing teachings, interviews, and discussions.</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Structure and Operations</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Local Chapters</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Meeting Spaces</strong>: Borrowed from community centers, churches, or homes.</li>
<li><strong>Facilitators</strong>: Trained volunteers who guide discussions and ensure a safe environment.</li>
<li><strong>Autonomy</strong>: Local groups operate independently but align with the core principles of The Freedom Center.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Governance</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Advisory Board</strong>: Composed of scholars, therapists, and community leaders to provide guidance.</li>
<li><strong>Core Team</strong>: A small group overseeing operations, resources, and partnerships.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Funding Model</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Donations</strong>: Voluntary contributions from participants (like AA/Al-Anon).</li>
<li><strong>Workshop Fees</strong>: Charging modest fees for courses or events to sustain operations.</li>
<li><strong>Grants</strong>: Seeking funding from foundations that support education, mental health, or spiritual growth.</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Branding and Identity</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Logo and Visuals</strong>:
<ul>
<li>A logo that combines elements of openness (e.g., an open door, a tree, or a circle) with symbols of growth and exploration.</li>
<li>A color palette that feels warm, inviting, and modern (e.g., earthy tones with accents of blue or gold).</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Tone and Voice</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Conversational</strong>: Approachable and inclusive, avoiding overly academic or religious jargon.</li>
<li><strong>Empowering</strong>: Encouraging curiosity, growth, and self-discovery.</li>
<li><strong>Hopeful</strong>: Emphasizing freedom, transformation, and community.</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>First Steps to Launch</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Pilot Program</strong>:
<ul>
<li>Start with a small group (in-person or online) to test the format and gather feedback.</li>
<li>Focus on one or two core offerings (e.g., a Bible Exploration group and a Critical Thinking workshop).</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Build a Team</strong>:
<ul>
<li>Recruit facilitators, therapists, and scholars who share your vision.</li>
<li>Train them in the principles and practices of The Freedom Center.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Create Resources</strong>:
<ul>
<li>Develop materials for Bible study, critical thinking, and emotional growth.</li>
<li>Record introductory videos or podcasts to explain the vision and invite participation.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Promote the Initiative</strong>:
<ul>
<li>Use social media, local networks, and partnerships to spread the word.</li>
<li>Host a launch event or open house to introduce The Freedom Center to the community.</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Long-Term Vision</strong></h3>
<p>The Freedom Center could grow into a network of local and online communities, offering a unique blend of spiritual, intellectual, and emotional support. Over time, it could become a movement that helps people engage with ancient wisdom, develop critical thinking skills, and grow in emotional intelligence—all within a supportive, non-dogmatic community.</p>
<p>By staying true to your core values and fostering a culture of curiosity and compassion, The Freedom Center could become a transformative force in the lives of countless individuals.</p>
<!-- notionvc: 7361458a-6511-4909-9344-c143afdee8b3 --></div><div><br /></div><div><br /></div><div><h4 style="text-align: left;">AI Response (DeepSeek) - 1:</h4><div><p>Your vision is both ambitious and deeply meaningful, blending spiritual exploration, intellectual growth, and emotional support into a cohesive community experience. To organize this into something coherent, let’s break it down into core components, principles, and actionable steps, drawing inspiration from the structure of groups like Al-Anon or AA while tailoring it to your unique goals.</p>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Core Principles and Practices</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Shared Practices and Principles</strong>&nbsp;(like AA/Al-Anon):
<ul>
<li><strong>Non-dogmatic Approach</strong>: Avoid rigid doctrines, creeds, or "we believe" statements. Focus on exploration, questioning, and personal growth.</li>
<li><strong>Community-Centered</strong>: Emphasize shared learning and mutual support rather than hierarchical leadership.</li>
<li><strong>Anonymity and Safety</strong>: Create a safe space where people can share openly without fear of judgment or proselytization.</li>
<li><strong>Voluntary Participation</strong>: No pressure to conform or commit; people can engage as much or as little as they feel comfortable.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Biblical Engagement</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Contextual Reading</strong>: Teach participants to read the Bible through the lens of its Ancient Near Eastern (ANE) and Second Temple worldview.</li>
<li><strong>Wisdom Meditation</strong>: Approach the text as meditative wisdom literature rather than prescriptive law.</li>
<li><strong>Theological Wrestling</strong>: Encourage deep, honest engagement with the text and its implications for modern life.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Critical Thinking and Emotional Growth</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Critical Thinking Skills</strong>: Teach participants how to analyze ideas, ask questions, and engage in constructive dialogue.</li>
<li><strong>Emotional Intelligence</strong>: Foster curiosity, empathy, and the ability to engage without defensiveness.</li>
<li><strong>Therapeutic Support</strong>: Offer group sessions that blend spiritual and emotional growth, with referrals to licensed therapists when needed.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Holistic Integration</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Interdisciplinary Approach</strong>: Combine insights from academic biblical studies, philosophy, theology, and psychology.</li>
<li><strong>Practical Application</strong>: Help participants apply ancient wisdom to contemporary challenges in a thoughtful, nuanced way.</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Structure and Format</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Local Groups</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Meeting Spaces</strong>: Borrow space from community centers, churches, libraries, or even homes (like AA/Al-Anon).</li>
<li><strong>Regular Gatherings</strong>: Weekly or bi-weekly meetings with a flexible structure (e.g., discussion, meditation, teaching, or group therapy).</li>
<li><strong>Facilitators</strong>: Trained lay leaders who guide discussions and ensure a safe, respectful environment.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Program Offerings</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Bible Study Groups</strong>: Focused on contextual reading and wisdom meditation.</li>
<li><strong>Critical Thinking Workshops</strong>: Teach skills for analyzing ideas and engaging in dialogue.</li>
<li><strong>Therapeutic Groups</strong>: Facilitated discussions on emotional and spiritual growth, with referrals to licensed therapists as needed.</li>
<li><strong>Guest Speakers</strong>: Invite scholars, therapists, or theologians to share insights on specific topics.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Online Presence</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Resource Hub</strong>: Offer articles, videos, and courses on biblical studies, critical thinking, and emotional growth.</li>
<li><strong>Virtual Meetings</strong>: Allow people to participate remotely, fostering a broader community.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Partnerships</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Therapists</strong>: Collaborate with licensed professionals to provide clinical support when needed.</li>
<li><strong>Academic Institutions</strong>: Partner with seminaries or universities to offer accredited courses or certifications.</li>
<li><strong>Existing Organizations</strong>: Work with groups like Fuller Theological Seminary or local churches to expand reach and resources.</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Name and Identity</strong></h3>
<p>Your initiative needs a name that reflects its purpose and values. Consider something that evokes exploration, community, and growth. Examples:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>The Wrestling Ground</strong>: Emphasizes the idea of wrestling with God and ideas.</li>
<li><strong>Wisdom Commons</strong>: Highlights the communal pursuit of wisdom.</li>
<li><strong>Open Table</strong>: Suggests inclusivity and shared exploration.</li>
<li><strong>The Thinking Faith Collective</strong>: Combines intellectual and spiritual growth.</li>
</ul>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Governance and Funding</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Governance</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Local Autonomy</strong>: Like AA/Al-Anon, allow local groups to operate independently while adhering to core principles.</li>
<li><strong>Advisory Board</strong>: Form a board of scholars, therapists, and community leaders to provide guidance and oversight.</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li><strong>Funding</strong>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Donations</strong>: Rely on voluntary contributions from participants (like AA/Al-Anon).</li>
<li><strong>Grants</strong>: Seek funding from foundations that support education, mental health, or spiritual growth.</li>
<li><strong>Workshops/Courses</strong>: Offer paid courses or certifications to sustain the initiative.</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>First Steps</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Define Your Vision</strong>: Write a clear mission statement and core principles.</li>
<li><strong>Start Small</strong>: Launch a pilot group in your local area or online.</li>
<li><strong>Build a Team</strong>: Recruit facilitators, therapists, and scholars who share your vision.</li>
<li><strong>Create Resources</strong>: Develop materials for Bible study, critical thinking, and emotional growth.</li>
<li><strong>Promote the Initiative</strong>: Use social media, local networks, and partnerships to spread the word.</li>
</ol>
<hr />
<h3><strong>Long-Term Vision</strong></h3>
<p>Over time, your initiative could grow into a network of local and online communities, offering a unique blend of spiritual, intellectual, and emotional support. By staying true to your core principles and fostering a culture of curiosity and compassion, you could create a movement that helps people engage with ancient wisdom in a way that is both authentic and transformative.</p>
<p>This is a big idea, but it’s also deeply needed in today’s world. By starting small and staying focused on your vision, you can build something truly impactful.</p>
<!--notionvc: 1eb062a6-c49f-47de-9257-785e463739f6--></div>
<div><br /></div><div><br /></div><div><br /></div><h4>AI Prompt (DeepSeek):</h4><div>Background: Al-Anon/AA/NA/CoDA/Celebrate Recovery all operate on a similar principle. The meeting location is largely irrelevant (wherever space can be found), but there is shared set of practices and principles upon which they internally operate, and most of them share a large overlap of those even with each other. They aren't a "Church", they don't own buildings, they don't even have LLCs in most cases, although they have some governance locally and nationally (albeit loosely).&nbsp; &nbsp;-----&gt; In much the same way, I'm feeling an increasing desire over the last decade to start.... SOMETHING?.... related to both helping people read the bible more authentically (free from doctrines, creeds, and traditions, and 'we believe' commitments) while also providing a place for people to think better. Learn critical thinking skills, how to get curious and not defensive, etc. How to understand the biblical authors ANE/2nd Temple worldview, what it means to read the bible as wisdom meditation literature and not "law", and then think about that wisdom in today's context theologically. Not stripping words and blindly applying them to today, but really "wrestling with God" as Jacob is said to have done, and do so in community. I would also like to offer a variety of group sessions that border on therapy or at least therapeutic, and partner with licensed therapists for those who need clinical assistance, while offering (very well trained) "lay counseling", in line with Siang-Yang Tan's philosophy. In some ways, Academic Biblical Studies, Critical Thinking courses, Philosophy, Theology, and Psychology seem a disparate group of things to homogenize into a single (?Group, Organization, Event, Label?). But in other ways, it seems natural, and indeed Fuller Theological Seminary blends many Theology/Psychology programs. Help me organize this thought into something coherent.</div>
